content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COIviMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
October 8, 1998
The Honorable Carl Washington
California State Assembly
145 East Compton Blvd.
Compton, California 90220
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-221
Dear Assemblymember Washington:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Ifthe California Department of Transportation (“Cal Trans”) erects a highway sign
stating that Assemblyman Carl Washington participated in the Cal Trans Adopt-a-Highway
program (“program”), is the sign considered a contribution or a gift to you?
CONCLUSION
The sign, which would only acknowledge your volunteer service to the program, is
neither a contribution nor a gift to you.
FACTS
You are a member of the California Assembly from the Fifty-Second Assembly District.
For many years, you have been actively involved in urging Cal Trans to remove litter from
highways in your district and to maintain the highways in a generally clean condition.
Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-221
Page 2
Recently, Cal Trans contacted your office about the possibility of your participation in
the Cal Trans Adopt-a-Highway program. Under this program, individuals or organizations,
including government agencies, donate their time or money to assist in beautification projects for
a portion of a specific highway. The projects generally include litter, graffiti or weed removal or
the planting oftrees and plants. In recognition of this, Cal Trans places signs that acknowledge
the volunteer service along the portion ofhighway “adopted” by the individual or organization.
Your employees and you would like to participate in the program by volunteering your
time to assist in some or all of the activities in relation to a portion of a specific highway in your
district. The time would be volunteered outside ofnormal Assembly work hours and would
consist of a few hours on Saturdays at four different times during a calendar year. Your staffs
participation in the program would not be a condition of employment in your office. In
recognition of this service, and in accordance with the usual procedure followed by Cal Trans in
administering this program, Cal Trans would erect one or more signs along the portion of
highway that you have “adopted.” Each sign would indicate the type of service being donated,
i.e., “Litter Removal,” indicate that it is part ofthe Adopt-a-Highway program, and recognize
you as the donor by printing your name and office, “Assemblyman Carl Washington.” There
would be no other reference to you on the sign.
ANALYSIS
Contribution
The Act defines a “contribution” as a payment, except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received, unless it is clear from the circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes. (Section 820 15(a).) The sign is considered a “payment”
2 from Cal Trans.
Under Regulation 182 15(a) (copy enclosed), a payment is made for political purposes if it
is:
“(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any
measure; or
(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any
agent thereof:
(A) A candidate;
(B) A controlled committee;”...
2 ‘“Payment’ means a payment, disthbution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of
money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible.” (Section 82044.)


File No. A-98-221
Page 3
Pursuant to Regulation 18215(c)(4), a contribution does not include:
“(4) A payment made at the behest of a candidate, which is for a
communication by the candidate or any other person, that meets all
of the following:
(i) Does not contain express advocacy;
(ii) Does not make reference to the candidate’s candidacy for
elective office, the candidate’s election campaign, or the
candidate’s or his or her opponent’s qualifications for office; and
(iii) Does not solicit contributions to the candidate or to third
persons for use in support ofthe candidate or in opposition to the
candidate’s opponent.”
According to the facts of your situation, the Cal Trans sign is only an acknowledgment of
your volunteer service to the program. As long as the sign does not contain express advocacy,
make reference to your candidacy or solicit contributions, the sign is not a contribution to you.
Gift
Section 82028(a) defines a “gift” as:
“[Amy payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient,
to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything
of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course
ofbusiness to members of the public without regard to official
status. Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by reason ofreceipt of
consideration has the burden ofproving that the consideration
received is of equal or greater value.” (Emphasis added.)
The emphasized language, “confers a personal benefit on the recipient,” was added last
year. (Stats. 1997, c. 450 (S.B. 124), § 2, eff. Sept. 24, 1997.) After the amendment, a payment
is a gift only if (among other things) it “confers a personal benefit” on the recipient.
(See discussion in Yee Advice Letter, No. A-98-197, copy enclosed.)
We have advised that a payment “confers a personal benefit” within the meaning of
Section 82028(a) in circumstances such as the following:
1)
The payment is earmarked for the use of a particular public official, or a group ofpublic
officials (contrast payments which support the ordinary operations of an agency).


File No. A-98-221
Page 4
2)
The payment provides the public official with a benefit such as more comfortable travel
arrangements (i.e., a business class ticket instead of coach, or a more expensive hotel)
than he or she would have enjoyed but for the payment.
The sign does not confer a personal benefit on you and it is in accordance with the
program procedure to acknowledge the volunteer service that an individual or organization
provides. Accordingly, a sign that acknowledges your volunteer service to the program is not a
gift to you.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
JI1J Stec er
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JS :tls
Enclosures

neither a contribution nor a gift to you.

QUESTION: Ifthe California Department of Transportation (“Cal Trans”) erects a highway sign
stating that Assemblyman Carl Washington participated in the Cal Trans Adopt-a-Highway
program (“program”), is the sign considered a contribution or a gift to you?

CONCLUSION: The sign, which would only acknowledge your volunteer service to the program, is
neither a contribution nor a gift to you.
